title: Pipe and Filter
description:
Pipe-and-Filter architecture organizes the system as a sequence of independent processing steps (filters), connected by communication channels (pipes). Each filter receives input data, performs a transformation, and produces output that is passed to the next stage, forming a continuous processing flow. This approach is common in data processing systems, compilers, and transformation pipelines. Its organization is based on the following conceptual blocks:
[ul]
[b]Filters[/b]: Components responsible for processing input data and generating output data, without direct knowledge of the data's origin or final destination. Each filter performs a specific, well-defined task.
[b]Pipes[/b]: Structures responsible for transporting data between filters, ensuring sequential or streaming information flow.
[b]Data Flow[/b]: Defines the execution order of filters, which can be linear or more complex depending on pipeline composition.[/ul]
This architecture offers advantages such as: [b]good maintainability[/b], as filters can be modified or replaced in isolation; [b]good performance[/b], especially in streaming or parallel processing scenarios; and [b]high compatibility[/b], because different filters can be developed in different technologies as long as they respect the input and output format.
Disadvantages include [b]low reliability[/b], as a failure in one filter can stop the entire pipeline; and [b]low usability[/b], since this architecture is not naturally geared toward end-user interaction, being more suitable for internal data processing.
concept: Sequence of transformations where the output of one filter is the input of the next through 'pipes'.
advantage: Stream Processing. Excellent performance for bulk data and high modularity.
disadvantage: Limited Interactivity. Low usability for the end user, focused on internal processing.

bullet_point.bullet3: Data flows through a continuous sequential stream
